JD SPORTS CANADA // MERCHANDISING

Online Shopping Market Pricing

Google Merchant Centre · Window: Mar 25 – Apr 21, 2026 · Generated Apr 22, 2026
Overall Position vs. Market
JD Sports Canada Catalogue — Pricing Split
About this report

This report brings together three Google Merchant Centre exports into a single view of JD Sports Canada's competitive pricing position. Every SKU is compared to its Market Price (the click-weighted median competitor price on Google Shopping) to identify where we're over- or under-priced relative to the market. For ~17% of SKUs Google has also run an AI-powered Discount Price Simulation, modelling how clicks and conversions would grow if we moved to a specific discounted price. The report is designed for weekly use by the merchandising team — filter, search, and click any row for a visual price comparison.

Dynamic SKU Pricing
84.5% of titles have multiple Market Prices across variants. Always work at Product ID level, not Title level.
A single style — e.g. Jordan 1 Mid SE — may carry 14 Product IDs (different sizes and colourways), each with a distinct competitive market price ranging from $140 to $401. This is how the resale and sneaker market works: size and colourway scarcity drives wildly different demand, and Google's benchmark reflects that. A "Nike Air Force 1 '07" sits at one market price for the common colourway and a very different one for a limited drop — treating them as one product would produce misleading pricing decisions.
Your Price
Current JD Sports Canada selling price for this Product ID (variant SKU).
Market Price
The typical competitor price on Google Shopping — a click-weighted median of what customers actually pay at competing retailers. The hero price for decision-making. Unique per Product ID.
Price Gap %
(Your Price − Market Price) ÷ Market Price. Positive = priced above market (red). Negative = priced below market (green). At market = within ±2%.
Discount Price Simulation
Google's AI simulation of performance at a discounted price — NOT a recommendation. A thought exercise showing predicted click / conversion growth if we ran that discount.
Simulation Confidence
Google's confidence in its own simulation: High, Medium, Low. Based on data volume and signal strength for that product.
Click / Conv. Uplift %
Predicted percentage growth (not multiplier) in clicks and conversions at the simulated discount price. e.g. +198% means clicks nearly triple.
Opportunity

Product Brand Your Price Market Price Gap % Clicks (28d) Simulation Sim. Price Confidence Click Uplift Conv. Uplift
Brand-level split of SKU pricing position against Market Price. The bars show the proportion of each brand's SKUs that sit below, at, or over market. Longer green means the brand is systematically underpriced (margin opportunity); longer red means systematically overpriced (demand risk). Click any brand to jump to its filtered Pricing Opportunity view.
Below Market At Market (±2%) Over Market
Data notes. Market Price = Google click-weighted median competitor price, unique per Product ID to reflect size and colourway scarcity. Simulation coverage is approximately 17% of the catalogue; only SKUs with a simulation show uplift columns. Uplift values are percentage growth (Google's native format), not multipliers — e.g. +198% means clicks nearly triple. "Your price" sourced from the Price Benchmarks export (canonical); the Sale Price Suggestions export showed 1,249 price discrepancies, reconciled to the Benchmarks value. 1,249 Google sale-price suggestions reference Product IDs that are not present in the current Price Benchmarks export and are excluded from this view — likely delisted SKUs or variants that fell out of Google's benchmark tracking this window. Single-product elasticity — Google's simulations do not model cannibalisation between our own SKUs, so sum-of-simulations should be treated as a ceiling. Last-click Google Shopping only — Meta and Klaviyo contribution to the same purchases is not visible here.
